From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Main administrative seat held by a bishop

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Roma-san_giovanni03.jpg/250px-Roma-san_giovanni03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roma-san_giovanni03.jpg)

The seat or *cathedra* of the Bishop of Rome in the [Archbasilica of Saint John Lateran](/wiki/Archbasilica_of_Saint_John_Lateran "Archbasilica of Saint John Lateran")

An **episcopal see** is the area of a [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop")'s [ecclesiastical jurisdiction](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_jurisdiction "Ecclesiastical jurisdiction").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Phrases concerning actions occurring within or outside an episcopal see are indicative of the geographical significance of the term, making it synonymous with *[diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese")*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The word *see* is derived from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *sedes*, which in its original or proper sense denotes the seat or chair that, in the case of a bishop, is the earliest symbol of the bishop's authority.[[7]](#cite_note-ODCC-7) This symbolic chair is also known as the bishop's *[cathedra](/wiki/Cathedra "Cathedra")*. The church in which it is placed is for that reason called the bishop's [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral"), from Latin *ecclesia cathedralis*, meaning the 'church of the *cathedra*'. The word *throne* is also used, especially in the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), both for the chair and for the area of ecclesiastical jurisdiction.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The term *see* is also used of the town where the cathedral or the bishop's residence is located.[[7]](#cite_note-ODCC-7)

## Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Episcopal_see&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Catholic Church")]

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Papal_primacy,_supremacy_and_infallibility "Template:Papal primacy, supremacy and infallibility") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Papal_primacy,_supremacy_and_infallibility "Template talk:Papal primacy, supremacy and infallibility") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Papal_primacy,_supremacy_and_infallibility "Special:EditPage/Template:Papal primacy, supremacy and infallibility") |

Within Catholicism, each diocese is considered to be a see unto itself with a certain allegiance to the See of Rome. The idea of a see as a sovereign entity is somewhat complicated due to the existence of the twenty-three [Particular Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"). Both the Western Church and its Eastern Catholic counterparts reserve some level of autonomy, yet each also is subdivided into smaller sees (dioceses and archdioceses). The episcopal see of the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope"), the bishop of Rome, is known as "the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See")"[[9]](#cite_note-9) or "the [Apostolic See](/wiki/Apostolic_see#Rome_as_the_Apostolic_See "Apostolic see")",[[10]](#cite_note-10) claiming [papal supremacy](/wiki/Papal_supremacy "Papal supremacy").

## Eastern Orthodox Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Episcopal_see&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox Church")]

The [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") views all [bishops](/wiki/Bishop_(Eastern_Orthodox_Church) "Bishop (Eastern Orthodox Church)") as sacramentally equal, and in principle holding equal authority, each over his own see. Certain bishops may be granted additional administrative duties over wider regions (as in the idea of the [Pentarchy](/wiki/Pentarchy "Pentarchy")), but these powers are limited and never extend over the entire Church. Thus, the Eastern Orthodox [oppose the idea of papal supremacy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_opposition_to_papal_supremacy "Eastern Orthodox opposition to papal supremacy") or any similar supremacy by any one bishop.

## United Methodist Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Episcopal_see&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: United Methodist Church")]

The [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") is divided into [Annual Conferences](/wiki/Annual_conferences_within_Methodism "Annual conferences within Methodism"), each one of which is presided over by a resident bishop, who is [bishop](/wiki/Bishop#Methodism "Bishop") of a named [Episcopal Area](/wiki/Episcopal_Area "Episcopal Area"), or See city. This is usually the Annual Conference's largest, or sometimes most centrally located, city. Annual Conferences are the regional bodies which are the fundamental basic bodies of which the [United Methodist Global Connection](/wiki/General_Conference_(Methodism) "General Conference (Methodism)") is composed. Annual Conferences are responsible for many matters, including the approval, election and ordination of clergy, who then become members of the Annual Conference in which they are elected and ordained and – with some exceptions – serve within the bounds of for the tenure of their ministries.

[United Methodist bishops](/wiki/Bishop#Methodism "Bishop") are elected in larger regional conclaves every four years which are known as [Jurisdictional Conferences](/wiki/Jurisdictional_conferences_(United_Methodist_Church) "Jurisdictional conferences (United Methodist Church)"). These super-regional Jurisdictional Conferences comprise an equal number of lay and clergy delegates from each Annual Conference, each delegation determined by the size of the Annual Conference, within the Jurisdiction, and new bishops are elected and consecrated from among the clergy of the Jurisdiction's Annual Conferences. These bishops who are elected for life, are then sent to lead the various Annual Conferences of the Jurisdiction. Episcopal candidates are usually – although not always – the first clergy delegate elected from a particular Annual Conference. Each bishop is assigned to and leads for four year terms an Episcopal area, or see, of each Annual Conference. An Episcopal area can also comprise more than one Annual Conference when two smaller Annual Conferences agree to share a bishop.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
